17: The Lord's Supper dispute (1 Corinthians 11:17-34) reveals a community fractured along social-class lines. In the Greco-Roman patronal meal pattern, the deipnon (dinner) involved social differentiation: the host and high-status guests ate first in the triclinium (dining room), while lower-status clients and dependents ate later in the atrium with inferior food. The Corinthian pattern appears to be: wealthier members ate early and privately (1 Corinthians 11:21: 'one person goes hungry'), leaving the poor waiting (1 Corinthians 11:33). Paul's rebuke is both theological (you shame the body of Christ) and social (you despise those who have nothing, 1 Corinthians 11:22).
1: Imitation of Paul as imitation of Christ: the imitation theme (mimēsis) was central to ancient pedagogy. The disciple learned by observing and reproducing the master's behavior. In the Jewish tradition, disciples walked behind their rabbi to learn his gait, mannerisms, and practice as well as his teaching.
2: Oral traditions (paradoseis): before the NT documents were written and circulated, the communities relied on oral paradosis (tradition, handed-on teaching). Paul's reference to traditions he delivered to the Corinthians reflects the early church's sophisticated tradition-transmission network.
3: Head (kephalē) in Greco-Roman culture: in Greek physiology (Plato, Timaeus 44D; Aristotle, On the Parts of Animals 2.7), the head was the ruling part of the body and the seat of reason and authority. In LXX usage, kephalē often translates rosh (head of a tribe, head of an army — a positional/authority meaning).
4: Veiling in Roman religious practice: Roman men covered their heads (capite velato) during sacrifice and prayer — an act of pietas visible in countless Roman sculptures and reliefs. This Roman practice is likely what Paul is correcting: men bringing Roman civic-religious customs into Christian worship.
5: Women's head-coverings in the ancient Mediterranean: Greek, Roman, and Jewish women typically covered their heads in public settings. The unveiled woman risked being read as sexually available or as a slave (who had no honor to protect). Paul's instruction aligns with cross-cultural Mediterranean honor conventions while affirming women's participation in prayer and prophecy.
6: Shaved head as shame marker: in the ancient world, shaving a woman's head was a punishment for adultery (Numbers 5:18 may imply this) and a mark of mourning. Tacitus (Germania 19) describes Germanic tribes shaving adulterous women's heads. The shame of the shaved head illustrates why a bare head dishonors.
7: Image and glory (eikōn and doxa): eikōn comes from Genesis 1:26-27 (humans made in the image of God). The word doxa (glory) reflects the Hebrew kavod — the manifest weightiness of a person's honor and status. Paul's argument is that the different glory-relations between man and God vs. woman and man call for different practices.
8: Woman from man (Genesis 2): the creation sequence of Genesis 2:21-22 (woman formed from man's rib) is the scriptural basis. Paul is reading Genesis 2 as establishing the relational orientation of the first couple — not as establishing hierarchy of value but of derivation.
9: Made for man — Genesis 2:18: the Hebrew ezer (helper, partner) of Genesis 2:18 is not a diminutive term — it is used for God himself as Israel's helper (Psalms 121:2). Paul's argument from creation purpose does not reduce woman to instrument but describes the original relational design.
10: Authority on her head because of the angels: the most debated phrase in the passage. Possible backgrounds: (1) Genesis 6:1-4 traditions about angels attracted by women's beauty; (2) Qumran texts (1QSa; 1QM) that excluded the physically impaired from holy assembly because of the angels present; (3) the idea that angels observe and report on worship (Psalms 138:1 LXX). The exousia (authority) on her head is best read as the covering that grants her the authority to pray and prophesy.
11: In the Lord (en kyriō) — mutuality: Paul's qualifier introduces the eschatological community where the creation-order asymmetries are being transcended without being abolished. The phrase en kyriō is Paul's standard way of marking the sphere where the new creation's logic applies.
12: All things from God: the theological anchor that closes the creation-order argument. God is the source of both man and woman; the relational ordering serves the community without establishing permanent inferiority. This is consistent with Galatians 3:28 (neither male nor female in Christ Jesus).
13: Judgment from the community: Paul appeals to the Corinthians' own sense of propriety (to prepon — what is fitting/seemly). This was a key concept in Stoic and Aristotelian ethics: decorum was what fitted the nature and situation of the actor.
14: Nature (physis) as teacher: the appeal to physis (nature) was common in Stoic argumentation — nature itself teaches what is right. Paul's use here is more like convention or universal human practice than philosophical nature-reasoning; what nature teaches is what all cultures recognize as normal.
15: Long hair as natural covering: the peribolaion (covering, wrap) of a woman's long hair as her natural head-covering makes a physiological argument: women's hair naturally grows longer, suggesting a natural provision for modesty. The argument was accessible without recourse to theology.
16: Universal church practice as the settling criterion: Paul closes the head-covering discussion not with a theological ruling but a sociological one. The churches of God everywhere follow this practice — the Corinthians are deviant, not exemplary. The appeal to universal custom (not just his own opinion) is a rhetorical move appropriate to a community that prized their distinctiveness.
18: Divisions (schismata) in the assembly: the word schisma (from schizō, to split) — same root as the English schism — appeared in 1:10 for the party-factions around apostolic names. Now it reappears in 11:18 for the social divisions that have infected the common meal. The word migrates from theological to social context.
19: Haireseis (factions/heresies): hairesis originally meant a choice or a school of thought (Josephus uses it for the Pharisees, Sadducees, and Essenes as three haireseis of Judaism — Antiquities 13.5.9). Paul uses it for the factions that reveal who is genuinely approved (dokimoi) — a testing-sieve function.
20: The Lord's Supper (kyriakon deipnon): the adjective kyriakos (belonging to the Lord) appears only here and in Revelation 1:10 (the Lord's Day, kyriakē hēmera) in the NT. It became the technical name for the Christian communal meal and eventually for the Eucharist (kyriakē in later Greek, dominica in Latin).
21: Social stratification at the meal: Roman dinner conventions divided guests by social rank. The triclinium (formal dining room) accommodated the host's inner circle with fine food and wine while lower-status guests received lesser portions in the atrium. The Corinthian Lord's Supper apparently reproduced this Roman social hierarchy within the congregation.
22: Shaming those who have nothing: the Greek phrase tous mē echontas (those who have nothing/nothing to bring) indicates poor members who came to the communal meal without provisions. In a patronage economy, bringing nothing marked one as a dependent; the host's early eating humiliated them in front of the whole assembly.
23: The tradition Paul received (paralambanō): Paul claims to have received the Supper tradition from the Lord himself (apo tou kyriou). This does not necessarily mean direct revelation; it may mean that the tradition ultimately derives from Jesus — received through the apostolic chain from its Lord-given origin.
24: In remembrance (eis tēn emēn anamnēsin): anamnēsis in Greek has a richer meaning than mere cognitive recall. In LXX cultic contexts (Numbers 10:10; Leviticus 24:7), the memorial was an action that re-presented the saving event before God, not merely brought it to mind. The Passover Haggadah's instruction (Mishnah Pesahim 1 Corinthians 10:5) that each generation must see itself as having come out of Egypt reflects the same participatory remembrance.
25: New covenant (kainē diathēkē): the phrase comes from Jeremiah 31:31-34, the promise of a new covenant written on hearts rather than stone. No other Jewish text before Paul explicitly calls the cup the new covenant in blood — this is a distinctively Christian theological formulation of the Last Supper tradition.
26: Until he comes (achri hou elthē): the eschatological orientation of the Supper — it is a meal between the first and second comings, combining memorial of the past with anticipation of the future. The verb katangellete (you proclaim) makes the meal itself an act of proclamation, not merely a private ritual.
27: Eating and drinking in an unworthy manner (anaxiōs): the manner Paul condemns is specified in the context: going ahead without waiting for others, eating too much while the poor go hungry, treating it as a private meal rather than a communal rite. The guilt of the body and blood is the gravity of treating the Lord's death carelessly.
28: Self-examination (dokimazō): the verb dokimazō (test, approve after testing) was used for assaying metal and examining candidates for public office. Applied to Lord's Supper preparation, it called for communal and relational self-scrutiny — am I treating my fellow believers as members of Christ's body?
29: Discerning the body (diakrinōn to sōma): the failure Paul identifies is not abstract theological misunderstanding but the concrete failure to see the poor members as part of Christ's body. The word sōma here most likely refers to the community body (as in 1 Corinthians 12:12-27) rather than primarily the eucharistic elements, though both meanings may be in play.
30: Weakness, illness, and death in the congregation: Paul attributes some physical suffering and death among the Corinthians to divine discipline connected to eucharistic abuse. This is not a general theological claim about illness but a specific pastoral diagnosis of a particular community. The category of divine discipline (paideia) for God's children is common in Jewish wisdom (Proverbs 3:11-12; Hebrews 12:6).
31: If we judged ourselves (diekrinomen): the conditional implies that self-examination before the Supper forestalls divine judgment. The judicial language (krisis, krina, diakrinō) dominates 1 Corinthians 11:28-32, framing the Lord's Supper as the setting for a preliminary verdict that shapes one's standing before the final judgment.
32: Discipline (paideia) vs. condemnation (katakrithōmen): the distinction between the Lord's corrective discipline of believers and condemnation with the world is a key pastoral category. Paideia (discipline, training) was the term for the formation of character through education and correction — the Father's loving investment in children's growth.
33: Wait for one another (ekdechesthe): the word ekdechomai (to wait for, receive, welcome) appears in Hebrews 10:13 and 11:10 for patient waiting for God's promises. Applied to the Lord's Supper, it calls for the kind of patient mutual attention that the Roman dinner-stratification had eroded.
34: The rest Paul will arrange in person: the phrase ta de loipa hōs an elthō diataxomai (the rest I will arrange when I come) implies that 1 Corinthians 11:2-34 does not exhaust the practical issues; Paul intends to handle additional matters face-to-face. This closing gesture maintains his apostolic authority over the community's worship practices.
